---
title: Wood ash as a soil amendment
date: 2024-02-24
tags: [gardening, woodworking, soil]
---

The ashes from my workshop stove are not waste. Clean hardwood ash from unpainted, unglued offcuts is a fine source of potassium and a useful way to raise the pH of acidic soil. I sift out the charcoal chunks and save the fine gray powder in a covered metal bucket.

Not every bed welcomes it. Blueberries, potatoes, and rhododendrons prefer acidic conditions, and even a thin dusting of ash can set them back for a season. I reserve ash for the brassica bed and the garlic patch, and I never exceed a handful per square meter per year.

I avoid ashes from plywood, painted scrap, or pressure-treated wood entirely, since they carry glues and metals I do not want in my food. When in doubt the ash goes to the landfill, not the garden.
